title: CROSS-GRN: Directionality-Aware Cross-Modal Attention for Signed Gene Regulatory Network Inference from Single-Cell Multi-Omics
description: We propose CROSS-GRN, a transformer architecture that introduces dual asymmetric cross-attention and cell-type conditioning to infer signed (activation/repression), directional gene regulatory networks from single-cell multi-omics data. While XATGRN has explored cross-attention for signed GRN inference from bulk RNA, CROSS-GRN is the first to combine asymmetric cross-attention with single-cell multi-omics integration and explicit cell-type conditioning, enabling context-specific regulatory network learning.
motivation: Current cross-modal attention methods for single-cell multi-omics (Cisformer, Attune) achieve strong performance in modality generation but use symmetric cross-attention that misses the inherent directionality of gene regulation. XATGRN recently proposed cross-attention for directionality-aware GRN inference, but operates on bulk RNA-seq with GNN architectures and lacks cell-type conditioning. GRNFormer and AttentionGRN use transformers for GRN inference but do not combine multi-omics integration with signed edge prediction and context-specific conditioning. There is a gap for methods that can infer signed, directional, context-specific GRNs from single-cell multi-omics data.
proposed_approach: An 8-layer transformer with dual asymmetric cross-attention between gene expression and chromatin accessibility. The architecture maintains separate query projections for regulatory sources (TFs) and targets to model directionality, uses cell-type embeddings to modulate attention weights for context-specific networks, and jointly trains for signed edge prediction (activation/repression) alongside expression prediction. The key innovation is the combination of asymmetric cross-attention, multi-omics integration, and cell-type conditioning—a combination not explored by XATGRN (bulk data, GNNs), GRNFormer (foundation model enhancement), or AttentionGRN (single-modality, unsigned).
key_innovations: Dual asymmetric cross-attention between expression and accessibility modalities, explicitly modeling TF→target vs target→TF relationships
Cell-type-conditioned attention modulation for learning unified, context-specific regulatory networks
Joint prediction of edge existence AND regulatory sign (activation vs repression) from multi-omics
Single-cell multi-omics integration (scRNA + scATAC) with transformer architecture—distinct from XATGRN's bulk RNA + GNN approach
hypothesis: Asymmetric cross-attention with cell-type conditioning will learn signed, directional regulatory networks from single-cell multi-omics that are more accurate and biologically interpretable than symmetric attention methods and single-modality approaches, achieving better GRN inference and meaningful variation across cell types.

confirming: GRN inference AUROC significantly exceeds scMultiomeGRN and AttentionGRN baselines (p < 0.05, paired t-test)
Forward (TF→target) attention significantly more predictive than reverse attention (t-test, p < 0.01)
Sign prediction accuracy significantly above random on validation set (binomial test, p < 0.001)
Model with cell-type modulation outperforms static model in ablation study (p < 0.05)
CROSS-GRN outperforms XATGRN when both applied to single-cell multi-omics data
Match or exceed Cisformer on cross-modal generation (Pearson r > 0.85)
refuting: Asymmetric attention provides no benefit over symmetric attention (Cisformer-style)
Sign prediction is no better than random or correlation-based baselines
Cell-type conditioning does not improve over cell-agnostic training

related_work.title: Cisformer: a scalable cross-modality generation framework for decoding transcriptional regulation at single-cell resolution
related_work.key_difference: Cisformer uses symmetric cross-attention for generation. CROSS-GRN introduces asymmetric attention for directional GRN inference, signed edge prediction, and cell-type conditioning.

related_work.title: Cross-modal contrastive learning decodes developmental regulatory features through chromatin potential analysis
related_work.authors: Yang, Y., Xie, C., He, Q., et al.
related_work.key_difference: Attune focuses on cell representation learning via contrastive learning. CROSS-GRN focuses on regulatory network inference with explicit directionality, sign modeling, and cell-type conditioning.

related_work.title: Cross-attention graph neural networks for inferring gene regulatory networks with skewed degree distribution
related_work.authors: Xiong, J., Yin, N., Liang, S., et al.
related_work.key_difference: XATGRN uses cross-attention for signed GRN inference but on bulk RNA-seq only with GNNs, and lacks cell-type conditioning. CROSS-GRN uses single-cell multi-omics (RNA+ATAC), transformer architecture, and explicit cell-type conditioning.

related_work.title: GRNFormer: A biologically-guided framework for integrating gene regulatory networks into RNA foundation models
related_work.authors: Qiu, M., Hu, X., Zhan, F., et al.
related_work.key_difference: GRNFormer integrates GRNs into foundation models for downstream tasks. CROSS-GRN infers GRNs de novo from multi-omics with signed edges and cell-type conditioning.

related_work.title: AttentionGRN: a functional and directed graph transformer for gene regulatory network reconstruction from scRNA-seq data
related_work.authors: Gao, Z., Su, Y., Tang, J., et al.
related_work.key_difference: AttentionGRN uses graph transformers on scRNA-seq with directed structure encoding. CROSS-GRN uses multi-modal asymmetric cross-attention with multi-omics integration, sign prediction, and cell-type conditioning.

related_work.title: Deep learning-based cell-specific gene regulatory networks inferred from single-cell multiome data
related_work.key_difference: scMultiomeGRN uses GCNs and focuses on TF-TF networks without sign prediction. CROSS-GRN uses transformers with asymmetric gene-peak attention and predicts signed edges.

related_work.title: SCENIC+: single-cell multiomic inference of enhancers and gene regulatory networks
related_work.key_difference: SCENIC+ relies on motif databases and separate analysis steps. CROSS-GRN learns end-to-end with neural attention and does not require prior motif knowledge.

related_work.title: SCRIPro: Single-cell and spatial multiomic inference of gene regulatory networks
related_work.key_difference: SCRIPro uses separate analyses per cell type. CROSS-GRN learns a unified model with cell-type conditioning, enabling transfer and comparison across contexts.

datasets: 10x Genomics Multiome PBMC (~10k cells, 10 cell types)
SEA-AD human brain atlas (~100k cells, subset)
Norman et al. K562 perturb-seq (ground truth)
baselines: XATGRN (BMC Bioinformatics 2025) - cross-attention for signed GRN (bulk)
GRNFormer (arXiv 2025) - GRN integration into foundation models
AttentionGRN (Briefings in Bioinformatics 2025) - directed graph transformer
expected_outcomes: Match Cisformer on cross-modal generation (Pearson r > 0.85)
10-15% improvement in GRN AUROC over scMultiomeGRN and AttentionGRN
Meaningful Jaccard variation (0.3-0.7) between cell-type-specific GRNs
Better perturbation prediction than unsigned methods
Statistically significant benefit of asymmetric over symmetric attention
novelty_statement: While cross-attention for GRNs has been explored by XATGRN (bulk RNA, GNNs) and transformer-based GRN methods exist (AttentionGRN, GRNFormer), CROSS-GRN is the first to combine: (1) asymmetric cross-modal attention between expression and accessibility, (2) explicit cell-type conditioning for context-specific networks, and (3) signed edge prediction in a unified framework for single-cell multi-omics GRN inference. This combination fills a unique gap not addressed by existing methods.

risk_mitigation.risk: Directionality and sign may be inherently ambiguous from observational data without perturbations
risk_mitigation.mitigation: Include perturbation dataset (Norman et al.) for validation; if sign prediction fails on observational data but works with perturbations, pivot to semi-supervised approach
